ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried nai bai (baby pak choy variant) with garlic. Closest parent 'stir-fried vegetable dish' (FOODON_03301710) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for nai bai specifically. 'Leafy vegetables (cooked)' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for stir-fried nai bai with garlic. SNOMED CT does not have a specific food concept for this dish.

main_ingredients: nai bai (baby pak choy / Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried nai bai with garlic is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one plate of cooked leafy greens as a shared side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Pak Choy (Brassica chinensis), cooked, boiled / stir-fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with HPB Singapore Foodo data for cooked Chinese leafy greens
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nai bai (奶白) is a cultivar of Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis closely related to pak choy. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pak choy (FDC ID 170398) was used as the primary nutrient basis. Values were cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo entries for stir-fried Chinese leafy greens to account for added oil and salt typical of restaurant stir-frying. Energy and macros adjusted upward from plain boiled pak choy to reflect approximately 5 g oil per 100 g cooked dish, consistent with standard stir-fry preparation. This is an adapted estimate; exact restaurant formulation may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for non-starchy cooked vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried nai bai with garlic. Nai bai is a non-starchy leafy Brassica vegetable. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values of 10–20 for cooked non-starchy vegetables including pak choy and similar Brassica species. A GI of 15 is used as a conservative mid-range estimate for this dish. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 3.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.14, rounded to 1.1. This is a low-GI, low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from stir-frying with salt and sauces
hypertension.guidance: Stir-fried nai bai with garlic contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g due to added salt during restaurant preparation; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, stir-fried nai bai with garlic has a negligible glycemic load and is an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, supporting blood glucose control when consumed as part of a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high potassium, and presence of folate and vitamin K from leafy greens
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried nai bai with garlic is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and provides potassium (252 mg), folate (46 mcg DFE), and vitamin C (26 mg), nutrients associated with cardiovascular protection; the dish is suitable for heart-healthy diets, though sodium content should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Nai Bai with Garlic (蒜炒奶白, Suàn chǎo nǎi bái) is a classic Chinese vegetable dish made from baby nai bai — a tender cultivar of Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis closely related to pak choy — stir-fried at high heat in a wok with sliced or slivered garlic and a small amount of vegetable oil. The dish is a staple of Cantonese and Singaporean Chinese cuisine and is commonly served as a shared side dish alongside rice and protein dishes in both home cooking and restaurant settings. Nai bai has a mild, slightly sweet flavour with a crisp-tender texture when properly stir-fried; the garlic imparts a fragrant, savoury aroma. The dish is minimally processed, retaining most of the vegetable's natural nutrients including vitamin C, vitamin A (as beta-carotene), folate, calcium, and potassium. It is naturally low in calories (approximately 62 kcal per 100 g), very low in carbohydrates (3.8 g per 100 g), and has a very low glycemic index (estimated GI ~15), making it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking weight management. The primary nutritional concern in restaurant preparations is sodium, which can be elevated due to added salt. The dish is vegan, gluten-free, and halal. At Din Tai Fung Singapore, this dish is prepared with carefully selected baby nai bai and is a popular vegetable option on the menu.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried nai bai with garlic?
Is nai bai the same as pak choy?
Is garlic nai bai good for diabetics?
Is stir-fried nai bai low carb?
What vegetables are served at Din Tai Fung Singapore?
How much sodium is in stir-fried Chinese vegetables?
What is the GI of stir-fried leafy greens?

confidence_reason: Nai bai is a cultivar closely related to pak choy and is not independently listed in most national food composition databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked pak choy (FDC ID 170398) with adjustments for added oil and salt typical of restaurant stir-frying, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for stir-fried Chinese leafy greens. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for non-starchy cooked Brassica vegetables. Confidence is medium because no exact database entry for stir-fried nai bai with garlic exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables', which is the correct classification for a stir-fried leafy green vegetable dish. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked pak choy data (FDC ID 170398) with upward adjustment for approximately 5 g vegetable oil per 100 g cooked dish and added salt, consistent with standard restaurant stir-fry preparation. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for a non-starchy cooked Brassica vegetable; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried nai bai with garlic was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked pak choy. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as leafy green vegetables do not naturally contain vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as vegetable oil used in stir-frying contains negligible trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for vegetable oils. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4), potassium_mg (252.0), calcium_mg (93.0), iron_mg (0.8), vitamin_c_mg (26.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (149.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.04), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.07), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.5), vitamin_b6_mg (0.14), folate_mcg_dfe (46.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (37.0), magnesium_mg (19.0), zinc_mg (0.2), selenium_mcg (0.6), water_g (88.5) are all adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked pak choy data. 9. Sodium of 280 mg per 100 g is an estimate reflecting restaurant stir-fry preparation with added salt; plain cooked pak choy has approximately 29 mg sodium per 100 g per USDA. 10. Price data from field survey data (SGD 17.32) preserved from input for reference but not used in nutritional analysis.
